coredata.dc:title: Engineering ZIF-67 with heteroatom-doped carbon dots for ultra-sensitive and selective detection of serum albumin in artificial body fluids

coredata.dc:description: The development of highly sensitive and selective biosensors is crucial for early disease, health monitoring, and treatment. In this study, Zeolitic Imidazolate Framework-67 (ZIF-67) functionalized with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CQDs) is designed for the ultra-sensitive and selective detection of serum albumin in artificial body fluids. The N-CQD@ZIF-67 (NCZ-67) composite material was synthesized through a facile and environmental friendly process, leveraging the unique photoluminescent properties of nitrogen-doped carbon dots and the robust structural framework of ZIF-67. The synthesized composite is characterized using FTIR, PXRD, UV–visible spectroscopy, photoluminescence spectroscopy, Raman, EDS, XPS, and HRTEM techniques. It serves as a highly efficient ‘turn-on’ sensor for detecting bovine serum albumin (BSA) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.2129 µM. The sensing mechanism operates via Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET). The fluorescent lifetime decay analysis of fluorophore (NCZ-67) revealed lifetimes of 2.77 μs and 2.11 μs with and without the analyte BSA, respectively, confirming the FRET mechanism. The high overlap integral value, 2.61 × 1010 indicates a significantly enhanced FRET efficiency. The Benesi-Hildebrand equation demonstrates a 1:1 interaction between the analyte BSA and the NCZ-67 sensor. Additionally, NCZ-67 shows excellence in detecting albumin in artificial body fluid samples, confirming the sensor’s ultra-sensitivity, robustness, selectivity and extending its applicability in clinical biochemical analysis. The recovery percentage of BSA in artificial body fluids is around 92–97 % further validating its potential for clinical use. The study underscores the potential of N-CQD@ZIF-67 in developing next-generation biosensors, particularly for the early detection and monitoring of liver and kidney-related disorders.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: N-CQD@ZIF-67: A sensor for serum albumin biomarker detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of highly sensitive and selective biosensors is crucial for early disease, health monitoring, and treatment. In this study, Zeolitic Imidazolate Framework-67 (ZIF-67) functionalized with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CQDs) is designed for the ultra-sensitive and selective detection of serum albumin in artificial body fluids. The N-CQD@ZIF-67 (NCZ-67) composite material was synthesized through a facile and environmental friendly process, leveraging the unique photoluminescent properties of nitrogen-doped carbon dots and the robust structural framework of ZIF-67. The synthesized composite is characterized using FTIR, PXRD, UV–visible spectroscopy, photoluminescence spectroscopy, Raman, EDS, XPS, and HRTEM techniques. It serves as a highly efficient ‘turn-on’ sensor for detecting bovine serum albumin (BSA) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.2129 µM. The sensing mechanism operates via Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET). The fluorescent lifetime decay analysis of fluorophore (NCZ-67) revealed lifetimes of 2.77 μs and 2.11 μs with and without the analyte BSA, respectively, confirming the FRET mechanism. The high overlap integral value, 2.61 × 1010 indicates a significantly enhanced FRET efficiency. The Benesi-Hildebrand equation demonstrates a 1:1 interaction between the analyte BSA and the NCZ-67 sensor. Additionally, NCZ-67 shows excellence in detecting albumin in artificial body fluid samples, confirming the sensor’s ultra-sensitivity, robustness, selectivity and extending its applicability in clinical biochemical analysis. The recovery percentage of BSA in artificial body fluids is around 92–97 % further validating its potential for clinical use. The study underscores the potential of N-CQD@ZIF-67 in developing next-generation biosensors, particularly for the early detection and monitoring of liver and kidney-related disorders.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Proteins are vital component of the human body with serum albumin being particularly significant due to its abundance in the blood plasma of mammals [1,2]. Serum albumin has a significant role in biological functions like maintenance of physiological homeostasis [3], regulation of oncotic pressure [4], binding of drugs [4], and transport of hormones [5], fatty acids [6], etc. As a result, it serves as a monumental biomarker for liver and kidney function and various other conditions such as nutritional status, inflammatory conditions, etc [7].

Bovine serum albumin (BSA), is a protein composed of 580 amino acid residues, 17 cysteine crosslinked residues, and 1 free cysteine moiety [8,9]. BSA is 75 percent structurally similar to human serum albumin (HSA), so it can be considered as an analog to HSA [2]. The meticulous and sensitive sensing of BSA is necessary for applications in pharmaceutics [8], diagnosis [10], food processing [8], etc. BSA is an important biomarker as the lower levels in the blood (hypoalbuminemia) are a sign of liver failure and chronic hepatitis while higher level (hyperalbuminemia) in the blood is accompanied by edema and shock [11]. Higher levels of albumin in the urine (albuminuria) is a condition of kidney failure in an individual and the excessive increase in the amount of albumin also is an indicator of diabetes mellitus [2]. Albumin can also be considered a crucial biomarker for epilepsy as abnormal levels of albumin may lead to epilepsy in humans [12]. So, the sensing and quantification of albumin in body fluids are crucial for the wellness of an individual.

Even though there are certain conventional methods such as immunoassays and electrophoresis for the sensing of albumin, they have certain limitations such as low sensitivity, specificity, cost inefficiency, and complexity [13,14]. Recent research has focused on developing more reliable and sensible detectors for sensing biomarkers such as albumin. The traditional methods can be replaced by optical biosensors, which play a key role in sensing biological and chemical substances that possess high selectivity [15], sensitivity [15], real, label-free detection, and cost-effectiveness [16]. However, the intrinsic fluorophores with low quantum yield, excitation, and emission in the protein biomarkers create an incompatibility for optical sensing [17]. Therefore, the fabrication of a fluorescent probe with desired qualities is essential [17].

Recently, luminescent metal–organic framework (MOF) based probes have gained popularity due to their tunable optical properties, making them ideal for sensing applications [18–20]. MOFs evolve into suitable sensor material through tunable porosity, vast surface area, suitable host–guest interactions, good thermal stability, unique luminescence properties, magnetic properties, and electrical conductivity [21–25]. ZIF-67, a zeolite imidazole framework containing cobalt ions and the imidazole ligand is a distinct class in MOF that can form composites having desired luminescent, catalytic, and electrochemical properties by encapsulation of different materials or by doping [23,24]. Among various nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots also hold significant potential in sensing applications due to their tunable photoluminescence, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility [26,27]. Lin and co-workers developed BPEI-CQDs/ ZIF-8 as an ultra-sensitive, selective sensor for Cu2+ ions [18]. Encapsulating carbon dots within MOFs prevents aggregation, thereby enhancing their stability [28]. These studies highlight the potential of ZIF-67-based MOF composites combined with carbon nanomaterials for advanced sensing applications [29].

Recent research has shown that MOF composite as a promising material for sensing applications as they can efficiently tune the properties of conventional materials. Jiang and coworkers have reported that Gd-MOF composite that functions as an epileptic drug and sensor with higher detection efficiency than traditional probes [12]. Similarly, Tung et. al. synthesized a graphene-MOF composite for the detection of volatile organic compounds [30]. These studies confirm that a strategic combination of nanomaterials and MOFs can synergistically enhance the composite materials, making them suitable for sensor applications.

The current work focuses on the suitable combination of a MOF with a quantum dot. The study deals with developing an ultra-sensitive turn-on fluorescence sensor for bovine serum albumin (BSA) detection. The synthesized sensor integrates the desired properties of both ZIFs and N-CQDs offering high sensitivity, selectivity, stability, and cost-effectiveness compared to conventional materials. The sensor is synthesized using precursors N-CQDs and ZIF-67 and is further characterized by FTIR, UV–visible spectroscopy, PXRD, HRTEM, Raman, XPS, EDS, and Photoluminescence spectroscopy. The specificity of the sensor towards BSA is evaluated amidst other biomarkers and biologically relevant metal ions. The biological applicability of the sensor is further validated by the detection of BSA in artificial body fluids such as artificial blood and urine samples. Additionally, detailed investigations into the interactions between the sensor, NCZ-67, and the BSA provide insights into the mechanism of sensing and their binding interactions. Overall, this study demonstrates the potential of the developed sensor for real-world biomedical applications.

The synthesis and analysis were performed using chemicals of high purity (98–99 %). Cobalt acetate (Co(CH3COO)24H2O), 2-methyl imidazole (C4H6N2), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and citric acid (C6H8O7) were purchased from TCI Chemicals Pvt. Ltd. Ethylene diamine (C2H4(NH2)2), bovine serum albumin (BSA), urea (CO(NH2)2), sodium chloride (NaCl), and sodium phosphate (Na3PO4) were procured from Sisco Research Laboratories Pvt Ltd. Calcium chloride (CaCl2) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased from Central Drug House Pvt. Ltd, New Delhi.

The FTIR analysis was performed using a SUBTECH PEDS FTIR Spectrometer, at Department of Chemistry, University College, Thiruvananthapuram. The UV–visible spectrum was recorded using a UV–Vis-NIR-Spectrophotometer at CLIFF, Kariavattom. The PXRD patterns were obtained using the Rigaku-Miniflex Powder XRD Instrument at the Department of Chemistry, University College, Thiruvananthapuram. The solid-state photoluminescence spectra of the sensor were recorded using an Edinburgh FLS1000-SS-sm spectrometer at CLIFF, Kariavattom. The sensing experiments for the detection of albumin were conducted using Perkin Elmer FL 6500 spectrofluorometer at the Department of Chemistry, Mahatma Gandhi College, Thiruvananthapuram.

ZIF-67 was synthesized following a previously reported experimental method with some modifications [31,32]. Approximately 2 g of cobalt acetate and 4 g of 2-methyl imidazole were dissolved in 20 ml of distilled water with continuous stirring until fully dissolved. The cobalt acetate solution was added drop by drop to the 2-methyl imidazole solution with continuous stirring. To enhance the formation of ZIF-67, 1 M NaOH solution was added and the mixture was continuously stirred for 30 min leading to the precipitation of ZIF-67. The precipitate was filtered using a Whatman no. 41 filter paper and washed several times with distilled water. The washed precipitate was dried at 50 ℃ and stored in a desiccator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The nitrogen-doped carbon dots were synthesized by a microwave-assisted method with some modifications [31]. About 2 g of citric acid and 1.5 ml of ethylene diamine were mixed well in 20 ml DI water until it became a homogeneous solution. The mixture was microwaved at 600 W for 4 min. A yellowish-brown colour slurry of N-CQD was obtained which showed a bright blue emission under UV light. About 20 ml of distilled water was added and stirred well to make the slurry completely dissolve and centrifuged to obtain the homogeneous solution of N-CQDs.

The synthesized ZIF-67 and N-CQDs were combined in a ratio of 1:5 and the mixture was mixed vigorously well in a shaker for 30 min. The resultant precipitate was filtered using a Whatman filter paper no. 41 and washed with a minimum quantity of distilled water. The collected NCZ-67 was dried under an IR lamp and stored in an air-tight container.
The schematic representation of the synthesis of NCZ-67 is shown in Scheme 1 .

A paper strip was designed and developed to broaden the sensor's practical applicability to albumin detection. The paper strip was constructed using 2 × 6 cm Whatman filter paper no. 41 and coated with a slurry of the sensor, NCZ-67, and allowed to dry. The dried paper strips were then soaked in a 5 µM solution of BSA and observed under UV light.

Artificial urine was prepared by adding 0.48 g of sodium phosphate, 0.75 g of sodium chloride, 1.82 g of urea, and 0.45 g of potassium chloride in 50 ml distilled water. The pH of this mixture was then adjusted to match that of human urine (5.5–6.5) using 0.1 M HCl [33].

Artificial blood serum was prepared by adding 0.015 g of potassium chloride, 0.005 g of calcium chloride, and 0.36 g of sodium chloride in 50 ml of distilled water. The pH of this mixture was then adjusted as that of human blood serum (7.4) using 0.1 M NaOH solution [34].

20 mg of the synthesized NCZ-67 was accurately weighed. About 5 µM solution of various biomarkers such as BSA, creatinine, bilirubin, glucose, aspartic acid, vitamin C, urea, uric acid, glycine, folic acid, as well as biologically relevant metal solutions such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+ , and Zn2+ were prepared. The mixtures were continuously stirred for approximately 30 min. About 3 mL of the supernatant solution was collected and its photoluminescence spectrum was taken. The procedure was repeated for artificial body fluids to evaluate the sensor’s performance under biologically relevant conditions.

The FTIR spectrum was employed to analyse the functional groups in the prepared ZIF-67, N-CQD, and NCZ-67 composite materials. Fig. 1 shows the FTIR spectrum of the precursors ZIF-67, N-CQD, and the synthesized composite NCZ-67.

In the FTIR spectrum of ZIF-67, the peak at 445.56 cm−1 indicates the formation of Co-N bond [35]. The absence of a peak around 1849 cm−1 confirms the deprotonation of the ligand 2-methyl imidazole [35]. The peaks at 1142 cm−1 and 992 cm−1 correspond to the stretching and bending vibrations of the C-N bond [35]. The bands observed at 2924 cm−1 and 1449.29 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibrations of C-H and C=C groups present in ZIF-67 [36]. The peak at 1640 cm−1 is assigned to the C=N stretching of 2-methyl imidazole [35]. Additionally, the broad band at 3531 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibrations of O-H group [36].

The FTIR spectrum of the synthesized N-CQDs exhibits a strong band at 3252 cm−1, corresponding to N-H stretching, while a peak at 2985 cm−1 corresponds to C-H stretching. Bands at 1613 cm−1, and 1394 cm−1 corresponds to C=O stretching, a peak at 1053 cm−1 attributed to C-O stretching, and a peak at 1288 cm−1 is due to the bending vibration of C-N group [31,37].

The FTIR spectrum of the composite NCZ-67 displays characteristic peaks of both precursors ZIF-67 and N-CQDs confirming the successful formation of the composite. The peaks of ZIF-67 at 1578 cm−1 (C=N stretching of 2-methyl imidazole), 1146 cm−1 (C-N stretching), and 972 cm−1 (C-N bending) [38]. Additionally, the spectrum features N-CQDs, peaks at 3641 cm−1, 1647 cm−1, 1169 cm−1, and 757 cm−1 [38]. These peaks attributed to the O-H group, C=O group, C=C group, and C-O stretching of N-CQDs respectively [38]. The presence of these characteristic peaks confirms the successful integration of N-CQDs into ZIF-67 forming the NCZ-67 composite.

To assess the crystallinity and the crystallite size of the sensor, PXRD analysis was performed (Fig. 2
). The well-defined peaks in the PXRD patterns of ZIF-67 and NCZ-67 confirm the crystalline nature of both ZIF-67 and NCZ-67 samples [32]. The sharp peaks at 2θ values 7.3°, 10.32°, 12.71°, 14.67°, 16.44°, 17.99°, 22.14°, 24.46°, 26.67°, 29.68°, 30.68°, 31.42°, and 32.41° imputed to the reflections from the planes (011), (002), (112), (022), (013), (222), (114), (233), (134), (044), (334), (244) and (234) of ZIF-67 respectively [32]. The characteristic peaks of ZIF-67 are also present in the NCZ-67 which confirms the formation of composite [39]. N-CQDs exhibit a broader peak around 26° attributed to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon [40]. Similarly, ZIF-67 also shows peaks in the same region as N-CQDs, specifically around 25.19° and 25.61°. Upon integration of N-CQDs into ZIF-67, broader peaks were observed at 25.16, and 25.71° (Fig. S1), indicating the overlap of peaks from both ZIF-67 and N-CQDs in the XRD pattern, particularly around 25° which suggests the successful incorporation of the heteroatom-doped CQDs onto ZIF lattice. The degree of crystallinity for the composite, NCZ-67, is 83.91 % compared to 75.08 % for ZIF-67, indicating a high degree of crystallinity upon the formation of the composite. As the material shows a high degree of crystallinity, it can be a potential candidate for optical sensing applications [31]. The crystallite size of both NCZ-67 and ZIF-67 was calculated using the Debye-Scherrer equation,
D = 0.9 λ / β cos θ
[36]. The calculation of crystallite size distribution of both NCZ-67 and ZIF-67 is shown in Table S1. The crystallite size of the NCZ-67 is 38.24 nm, which is less than the crystallite size of ZIF-67, 43.96 nm. This reduction in crystallite size is attributed to the incorporation of N-CQDs, which may influence the growth and morphology of the composite.

The UV–visible spectra of ZIF-67 and NCZ-67 were recorded to understand their electronic transitions, as shown in Fig. 3 . In ZIF-67, the broad absorption maximum in the UV region (<400 nm) is attributed to the ligand–metal charge transition (LMCT) [41]. A characteristic absorption peak of ZIF-67 is found around 570 nm, attributed to the 4A2(F) → 4T1(P) transition by Co2+ ions [42]. The incorporation of N-CQDs into the ZIF-67 does not change the original composition of ZIF-67, as evidenced by the presence of a similar absorption peak in NCZ-67 in the range of 500 nm [42]. The hike in the absorption maxima may be due to the integration of N-CQDs in NCZ-67 [43]. The tauc plot in Fig. 4 , reveals two band gaps for NCZ-67 and ZIF-67 in the UV and visible regions. The band gap decreases from 3.44 eV to 3.35 eV in the UV region and from 1.84 eV to 1.83 eV in the visible region when transitioning from ZIF-67 to NCZ-67. The band gap reduction and the enhanced optical properties of NCZ-67 further confirm the successful incorporation of N-CQDs and their role in improving the composite’s optical behaviour [42].

Photoluminescence spectra of the synthesized ZIF-67, and NCZ-67 are shown in Fig. 5 a and 5b respectively. Upon excitation at 340 nm, NCZ-67 shows a strong emission peak, at 473.21 nm. While ZIF-67 have its emission at 436.34 nm, as shown in Fig. 5b. The incorporation of N-CQDs introduces additional surface states and energy levels within the band structure of the composite which leads to therecombination of electrons and holes resulting in stronger photoluminescence. The synthesized N-CQDs@ZIF-67 composite has significant enhancement in fluorescent intensity due to the synergistic effect of N-CQDs and thus the composite has great potential in optical sensing.

The XPS spectral studies are conducted to analyze the binding energies and electronic states of elements present in the synthesized composite NCZ-67. The XP survey scan and deconvoluted spectra of Co 2p, O1s, N1s, and C1s, for both NCZ-67 and ZIF-67, are shown in Fig. 6
( a -e), respectively.

Both NCZ-67 and ZIF-67 exhibited the same elemental composition, confirming their identical framework topology. However, the increased intensity in the deconvoluted spectra of NCZ-67 compared to ZIF-67 indicates an increase in the concentration of the constituent elements like carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen and an increase in surface concentration by the increased intensity of cobalt, due to the incorporation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots into the ZIF-67 framework. On incorporating N-CQDs there may be an increase in porosity [44] or any modifications in structure that lead to an increase in the surface area of the NCZ-67. The XPS spectra of cobalt exhibit two characteristic peaks at 784.46 eV and 794.75 eV corresponding to Co2p3/2 and Co2p1/2 respectively [45]. The other two peaks at 779.14 eV and 800.57 eV are assigned as the shake-up satellite peaks of Co2+ [45]. The deconvoluted spectra of N1s, C1s, and O1s each exhibit a single peak at binding energy values 396.81 eV, 282.75 eV, and 529.18 eV, respectively [46].

The energy dispersive spectroscopy analysis of the synthesized NCZ-67 provides the weight and atomic percentage of the elements C, N, O, and Co. The obtained EDS spectrum is shown in Fig. 7 , confirming the presence of these elements without any other impurities. Additionally, the analysis also ascertains that the elements' atomic and weight percentage, tabulated in Table 1 , reveal a significantly higher proportion of both carbon and nitrogen. This increase strongly supports the incorporation of an nitrogen-doped carbon dot in the ZIF-67 framework.

Raman spectroscopy, a non-destructive chemical analysis was employed to gain deeper insights into the chemical structure and molecular interactions. As, shown in Fig. S2, the Raman spectra of ZIF-67, show its characteristic peaks at 180, 268, 370, 419, 468, 510, and 670 cm−1.. The successful synthesis of ZIF-67 is confirmed by the peak at 419 cm−1, which is due to the Co-N bond [47]. The intense peak at 670 cm−1 corresponds to the 2-methyl imidazole [48]. Two Raman active modes of Co-O bond Eg and F2g give rise to the bands at 468 and 510 cm−1 [49]. The other peaks at the lower Raman shift value 180 cm−1, 268 cm−1, and 370 cm−1 are also attributed to the Co ions of ZIF-67 [48].

In the case of the synthesized sensor NCZ-67, the Raman spectrum shows distortions with noises due to the background signal from the fluorescence of the material. As the fluorescence signal from the sensor obscures the subtle peaks of Raman, proving the strong evidence of the incorporation of fluorescent nitrogen-doped CQDs within ZIF-67 can be affirmed.

The high-resolution TEM images taken by the interaction of the electron beam with the sample give insight into the morphology and crystal structure of the sensor NCZ-67. The Fig. 8 (a) and (b) shows the images at lower and higher magnification levels of 1 µm and 500 nm. From the images, it is clear that the nitrogen-doped CQDs are attached to the rhombic dodecahedral ZIF-67 [50]. The rhombic ZIF-67 is surrounded by the porous N-CQDs.

As BSA is a critical biomarker protein, its levels in body fluids can provide valuable insights into various health conditions related to kidney and liver function in humans. Elevated or reduced BSA levels are indicative of underlying medical issues, helping in the diagnosis and monitoring of diseases. Therefore, precise and highly sensitive detection of BSA is essential, yet conventional methods face numerous practical challenges. The study evaluates the use of an optical sensing technique for the ultrasensitive and selective detection of BSA using NCZ-67, along with other biomarkers and essential metal ions involved in vital human body functions.

The study assesses the NCZ-67′s selectivity using distinct biomarkers such as aspartic acid, glycine, folic acid, glucose, vitamin C, uric acid, creatinine, urea, bilirubin, and BSA, along with metal ions Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, and Zn2+ present in biological systems. Equimolar solutions (5 µM) of 50 mL of the specified biomolecules were prepared separately. Approximately 20 mg of NCZ-67 was added to each biomarker solution. The resulting mixtures were continuously vortexed for 30 min at room temperature. About 3 mL of the clear supernatant solution was carefully decanted from each mixture, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded. Notably, the NCZ-67 showed significant changes in the emission spectra, and the variation in fluorescent intensity as illustrated in Fig. 9 . The NCZ-67 exhibited a fluorescence enhancement (turn-on) when sensing BSA, with an efficiency of 67.68 %. At the same time, the fluorescent intensity curtailed on sensing other analytes, with quenching efficiencies, 15.20 %, 19.28 %, 21.77 %, 22.46 %, 26.36 %, 35.79 %, 45.88 %, 46.97 %, 47.73 %, 52.35 %, 54.04 %, 61.29 %, 64.68 %, 65.78 % for aspartic acid, glycine, folic acid, glucose, vitamin C, uric acid, K+, creatinine, Mg2+, Zn2+, bilirubin, Na+, Ca2+, and urea respectively. The sensing activity of the sensor towards BSA was studied in detail.

An efficient sensor should be able to sense the analyte even in the presence of other coexisting molecules and ions. To confirm the efficiency of the NCZ-67 sensor, sensing was conducted in a mixture containing BSA along with nine other biomarkers and five metal ions that are vital in human metabolic functions as shown in Fig. 10 . Even in the presence of other biomarkers such as aspartic acid, glycine, folic acid, uric acid, urea, creatinine, bilirubin, ascorbic acid, and glucose, the sensor demonstrated fluorescence enhancement specifically with BSA. Similarly, when probing a mixture of metal ions with BSA, the sensor exhibited a significant fluorescence enhancement. The anti-interference property of thesensor is confirmed by analyzing its sensing activity in a mixture of biomarkers with and without BSA. In contrast, the mixture with BSA shows an enhancement in FL intensity which is shown in Fig. S3. Since the sensor’s activity was unaffected by the presence of other biomarkers and ions, it can be effectively used for detecting BSA in body fluids.

For the in-depth analysis of the sensing activity of the sensor towards BSA, and to validate the method, the various concentrations of BSA ranging from 0.1 to 5 µM were analyzed by making a homogeneous solution with the sensor, thereby measuring its FL intensity. The enhancement of fluorescence intensity with an increase in concentration is shown in Fig. 11 , which confirms the ‘turn-on’ mechanism. The fluorescent intensity increases on the successful addition of BSA from 0.1 to 5 µM. The FL enhancement efficiencies were calculated for each incremental addition of BSA and found to be 45.28 %, 47.61 %, 49.75 %, 51.73 %, 53.01 %, 54.35 %, 59.35 %, 62.05 %, 64.03 %, and 67.68 % for the concentrations of 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9, 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 µM respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of BSA with variation of pH
ce:section.ce:para: The pH of the environment can influence the effectiveness of BSA sensing, as pH can affect both the structure of BSA and the sensor's performance. Therefore, studying the sensor's response to BSA across different pH levels is essential to ensure reliable detection under various physiological conditions.

The optimal pH conditions for the sensing can be derived by varying the pH of the mixture of the analyte and sensor from 2 to 14. The variation in PL intensity with pH is shown in Fig. 12 . The fluorescence enhancement efficiencies increase from acidic to neutral pH (2 to 7) and decline from neutral to basic pH (7 to 14). The analysis shows that the sensor exhibits a maximum fluorescence efficiency around pH 7 (67.68 %). The optimum pH for the sensing activity was found to be between 6 and 8, which is the pH range of human body fluids. At neutral pH, which closely resembles physiological conditions, the sensor displayed a stable and significant fluorescence enhancement. The NCZ-67 thus demonstrates effective BSA detection across various pH levels, with the best performance observed at neutral to slightly acidic pH. This pH-dependent fluorescence response highlights the sensor's robustness and suitability for detecting BSA in diverse biological environments.

The kinetics of BSA detection is crucial for understanding how quickly and efficiently a sensor can respond. Monitoring the sensor's fluorescence response over time provides insights into the dynamics of BSA binding and the overall performance of the sensor. The study focuses on the time-dependent sensing of BSA using the NCZ-67 sensor, highlighting how the sensor's fluorescence intensity changes as a function of time. The optimal time for sensing can be obtained by varying the time of interaction of the analyte and sensor from 5 to 30 min. The increase in FL intensity with time is depicted in Fig. 13 . As time progresses, the FL Intensity is enhanced. The fluorescence enhancement efficiencies calculated for the time intervals 5 min, 10 min, 15 min, 20 min, 25 min, and 30 min, are 51.26 %, 53.65 %, 56.95 %, 59.08 %, 59.93 %, and 59.95 % respectively. As the 25 and 30 min of interaction give almost similar fluorescence enhancement efficiencies, the optimal time of sensing would be around 25 min. The NCZ-67 exhibits a robust time-dependent fluorescence response to BSA, with significant fluorescence enhancement occurring rapidly after BSA introduction. The sensor's ability to reach a steady state within a short period indicates its efficiency in BSA detection. This time-dependent analysis confirms the sensor's potential for real-time monitoring of BSA in biomedical applications, offering both speed and reliability in performance.

The Stern-Volmer plot is plotted between relative intensity (I/I0) on the y-axis and the concentration of BSA solution on the x-axis, and is shown in Fig. 14 . The obtained plot is a straight line that indicates the linear relationship between the values of relative intensity and the concentrations of the analyte and the sensor, with an R2 value of 0.99536. The slope of the S-V plot will give Kenh, the enhancement constant which is calculated as 2.5893 × 106 M−1. As Kenh quantifies the efficiency of enhancement, a higher value of Kenh suggests the higher enhancement efficiency of the synthesized sensor towards the ultrasensitive detection of the analyte, BSA. The LOD value is calculated using the values of slope and intercept of the Stern- Volmer plot [36]. The calculation of the LOD was done using the equation, LOD = 3.3 × (Standard deviation of intercept/ Slope). The intercept showed a standard deviation of 0.01647. The sensor possesses a limit of detection of 0.2129 µM which is the lowest range of concentration of the analyte, BSA which can be detected by the NCZ-67 effectively. The lower value of LOD suggests the higher sensitivity of the sensor towards the analyte. Similarly, the sensor's capacity is also confirmed using the value of LOQ. The lower limit of quantification of the analyte is found to be 0.6450 µM, which projects the fact that, the sensor even can detect the analyte in very small quantities, which makes it a sensible sensor. The enhanced precision of the sensor can be confirmed by the LOD and LOQ values. The binding interaction of the sensor and the analyte was determined using the Benesi- Hildebrand equation [51] and it is found that the interaction of the analyte and sensor was in a 1:1 ratio as shown in Fig. S4 and S5. The binding constant (5.326 × 105 L/mol) and stoichiometry (0.1413) indicate strong sensor-analyte interaction, as evidenced by the high binding constant value.

The sensing mechanism of BSA using the NCZ-67 sensor can be attributed to the specific interactions between the sensor and the BSA molecules. Understanding the plausible mechanism behind this sensing process is crucial for optimizing the sensor's performance and ensuring its reliability in practical applications. The plausible mechanisms for fluorescent enhancement are often explained by Aggregation-Induced Emission enhancement (AIE), restriction of non-radiative pathways, chelation-induced fluorescent emission, hydrogen-bonded fluorescence enhancement, energy transfer mechanisms such as Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) and Photoinduced Electron Transfer (PET), etc.

To determine whether structural collapse occurs during the sensing of BSA, the PXRD patterns of the sensor were compared before and after sensing, as shown in Fig. S6. As there is no peak shift, additional peaks, or extinction of an existing peak, the possibility of structural collapse can be discarded, thus ruling out the possibility that the enhancement is by unintended structural degradation [52].

The FTIR analysis before and after sensing used to assess changes in the chemical structure or functional groups of the sensor and is shown in Fig. S7. As there are no significant changes before and after sensing, the chemical composition and functional groups of the sensor remain unchanged, indicating that it is chemically stable and does not undergo significant structural alterations during the sensing. This stability is crucial for ensuring consistent and reliable sensor performance over multiple uses. Hence, the fluorescence enhancement is likely by physical interactions or changes in the environment around the sensor rather than chemical alterations.

The UV–visible spectra show no significant change before and after sensing BSA (Fig. S8), indicating that the interaction between the sensor and BSA neither involves any significant alterations in the electronic structure of the sensor nor leads to the formation of a new absorbing species. The possibility of ground state complexation was thus ruled out.

To examine whether energy transfer mechanisms such as Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) and Photoinduced Electron Transfer, etc contribute towards the fluorescence enhancement, the absorption spectra of the analyte (BSA), and the absorption and emission spectra of the sensor, NCZ-67 were compared together as shown in Fig. 15 . The effective overlap of the absorption spectra of BSA and the emission spectra of the sensor suggests the possibility of FRET for the sensing of the analyte BSA. The overlap integral value was calculated using the equation [53],
J
λ
= F
λ
× ε
λ
×
λ
4
d λ
where F (λ) is the normalized sensor emission, ε (λ) is the molar extinction coefficient of the analyte and λ is the corresponding wavelength. The overlap integral, J (λ) calculated is 2.61 × 1010. The larger the overlap more efficient the energy transfer. The higher value of overlap integral depicts higher FRET efficiency.

Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC) is a highly sensitive technique used to measure fluorescence decay lifetimes with high temporal resolution. It is especially useful in studies involving Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET). Since there is a spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of analyte BSA and the absorption, emission spectra of NCZ-67 (Fig. 15), fluorescence lifetime decay studies were conducted and the fluorescence lifetime spectra of NCZ-67 and NCZ-67@BSA are shown in Fig. 16 . The time-resolved spectrum of fluorescence can provide the changes in the system under analysis [54]. The lifetime decay of the fluorophore without analyte and with analyte fit biexponentially with a lifetime of 2.77 μs and 2.11 μs respectively. The change in lifetime suggests the mechanism of FRET between the fluorophore and the analyte [55]. The decrease in lifetime can be correlated with increased FRET efficiency. As there is a significant difference in lifetime, the possibility of static turn-on can be ruled out and assure the possibility of dynamic turn-on. As the Inner Filter Effect (IFE) generally does not cause any change in the lifetime, the chance of the IFE can also be ruled out which is confirmed using the absence of overlap among excitation and emission spectra of NCZ-67 and theabsorption spectra of BSA as shown in Fig. S9. The energy transfer efficiency is calculated as 0.24 from the equation,
E = 1 - (
T T
0
)
where τ and τ0 are the lifetimes in the presence and absence of an analyte.

The reusability of the sensor is essential as the cost-effectiveness and minimum use of material are the critical qualities of a sensor. The reusability is checked by filtering and separating the sensor from the analyte after sensing. The reusability of the sensor after sensing BSA is shown in Fig. 17 . The sensor material is washed and reused for up to 4 cycles. There is only a small decrease in the FL intensity in 4th cycle which may be due to the loss of sensor material on washing several times. Hence, NCZ-67 can be used as a reusable turn-on sensor for sensing BSA in solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of NCZ-67 in sensing BSA in artificial body fluids
ce:section.ce:para: The clinical feasibility of the NCZ-67, in detecting biomarkers like albumin, involves evaluating its performance in complex biological environments. This includes testing it in artificial body fluids like blood serum and urine to determine their accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and practical utility in clinical settings. Artificial body fluids are often used to simulate the conditions of real biological fluids without the complexity and variability of actual samples. These fluids are designed to mimic the ionic composition, pH, and other properties of real body fluids. The use of NCZ-67 in these environments provides initial data on its potential clinical utility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of BSA in artificial blood serum
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of albumin in human blood is critical as the specific amount (3.4 to 5.4 g/dL) of albumin is the normal quantity needed in human blood for basic human function [6]. The lower or higher levels of albumin may lead to complications in daily functions and affect the health conditions of humans. The albumin levels in the blood resolve the possibility of kidney or liver diseases (lower albumin level) or conditions of dehydration (higher albumin level). To authenticate the sensor's practical applicability, reliability, and validity of the analyzing method, the sensing activity with the analyte is analyzed in the artificial blood serum. Different concentrations of albumin in artificial blood were mixed with the sensor and they exhibit a ‘turn-on fluorescence’ which makes the sensor reliable and biologically applicable. The added, found concentrations and recovery percentage of the analyte are in Table 2 . The average recovery percentage lies between 96–97 % indicating that the sensor can accurately detect and quantify BSA without significant loss. In blood serum, even with the complex matrix including proteins and electrolytes NCZ-67′s selectivity towards BSA enables effective sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of BSA in artificial urine
ce:section.ce:para: Sensing the presence of albumin (30–300 mg per day) in urine refers to the condition of microalbuminuria which is closely related to cardiovascular diseases and renal diseases in human [56]. The biological applicability of the sensor is ensured by the spiked analyte in artificial urine and the enhancement in the fluorescence intensity confirms the ‘turn-on’ mechanism. The added, found concentration, and recovery percentage in artificial urine calculated are shown in Table 3 in which the recovery percentage is around 92 %.
Thus, studies using artificial blood serum and urine have shown that NCZ-67 maintains its sensitivity and selectivity for albumin.

The robustness of the proposed method and sensor NCZ-67 was evaluated by introducing small variations in factors in key experimental parameters, including physiologically relevant pH and analyte concentration in artificial body fluids. The pH studies were conducted for the small range of 6.7–7.2 and 5.7–6.2 for artificial blood serum and artificial urine which is the optimal pH of blood and urine respectively. The effect of variation in analyte concentration was examined by adjusting the increments of 0.05 µM. The obtained results exhibited minimal standard deviation indicating the robustness and reliability of the proposed method for the biomarker sensing using NCZ-67.

The applicability of the sensor can be simplified by developing 2 × 6 cm NCZ-67 coated paper strips. The developed strips immersed into the BSA solution, exhibit an enhanced bright blue fluorescence under UV light compared to the strip without albumin. The visual detection using the paper strip is shown in Fig. 18 . With the analyte albumin, the blue fluorescence emission of the NCZ-67 sensor rapidly intensified under UV light. This visual change is easily detectable by the naked eye under UV exposure, making the sensor a promising candidate for albumin detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of various reported fluorescent probes in literature in sensing BSA
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of BSA by different fluorescent probes reported and their LOD towards the detection of BSA as per the literature is shown in Table 4 .

Comparing the LOD value with other published works, it is found that NCZ-67 shows remarkably significant sensing activity towards the sensing of BSA with the LOD value in a micromolar range which is comparatively lower than the many other reported works and is relatively closer to other works.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the sensing property of N-CQD@ZIF-67 with ZIF-67
ce:section.ce:para: To compare the sensing performance, experiments were conducted with ZIF-67, as shown in Fig. S10. While ZIF-67 also exhibits a turn-on response, its sensing efficiency for 5 µM BSA is 19.22 %, compared to NCZ-67′s 67.68 %. The enhanced efficiency of NCZ-67 is attributed to its stronger fluorescence response, additional functional groups for improved BSA interaction, and better energy transfer. This highlights the role of N-CQDs in boosting sensing efficiency, making NCZ-67 a superior fluorescent detector for BSA.

In this study, NCZ-67 sensor was successfully synthesized by incorporating nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) into the ZIF-67 framework. A comprehensive characterization was performed using FTIR, UV–vis spectroscopy, PXRD, XPS, EDS, Raman, HRTEM, and photoluminescence spectra confirming the structural integrity, elemental composition, and successful integration of N-CQDs. The study demonstrates the highly selective and sensitive detection of Bovine serum albumin using an NCZ-67 sensor. The selectivity of the material is tested in the presence of various vital biomarkers and metal ions present in body fluids. The material exhibits significant fluorescence enhancement upon interaction with the biomarker BSA with an efficiency of 67.68 %. The sensing activity of the sensor towards BSA was studied by varying time, pH, concentration, interference by other biomolecules of interest, etc. The Stern-Volmer analysis indicates a strong sensor-analyte interaction with a high enhancement constant (2.5893 x 106 M−1 and a detection limit of 0.2129 µM, which is in good agreement with previously reported fluorescent probes. From the Benesi −Hildebrand equation, the interaction of the sensor and analyte is found to be a 1:1 ratio with a binding constant of 5.326 × 105 L/mol. The proposed sensing mechanism suggests fluorescence enhancement arising from Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) between NCZ-67 and BSA, as evidenced by spectral overlap and time-resolved fluorescence decay studies. The sensor demonstrates excellent reusability, maintaining significant fluorescence activity over multiple sensing cycles, thereby proving its cost-effectiveness and sustainability.

The practical utility of NCZ-67 was further validated through the successful detection of BSA in artificial biological fluids such as blood serum and urine, with an adequate recovery percentage of around 92–97 %. With its high selectivity, sensitivity, and ability to function effectively in complex matrices, NCZ-67 thus emerges as a compelling choice for detecting BSA in artificial body fluids. Hence, NCZ-67 is a powerful and promising material for ensuring reliable and accurate detection of BSA with a high recovery percentage, holding significant potential in biomedical diagnostics and clinical applications. The findings pave the way for the development of advanced sensing platforms for the real-time monitoring of crucial biomarkers in complex biological systems.
